name: Scriptly & Hub Tasks – 30 Card Deck
description: A comprehensive flashcard deck covering Scriptly Task objects, task workflows, routing, templates, and operational rules within Walgreens Hub.

cards.question: What is the primary purpose of a Task in Scriptly?

a.text: To represent a discrete, trackable action needed to move a prescription or patient case forward.
a.explanation: Tasks are structured action items tied to workflow progression.

b.explanation: Insurance data is not stored as tasks.

c.explanation: Inventory is handled in dispensing systems.

d.explanation: Credentials are not managed via Tasks.

cards.explanation: Scriptly Tasks represent actionable steps required for workflow movement and operational tracking.

cards.question: Which business object defines preconfigured sets of steps for creating standardized tasks?

a.explanation: Templates define logic, routing, and fields for tasks.

b.explanation: Programs define therapy pathways, not task patterns.

c.explanation: Teams control permissions, not task structure.

d.explanation: Stages describe status, not template logic.

cards.explanation: Task Templates ensure standardized, repeatable task creation across workflows.

cards.question: When are Tasks automatically generated in Scriptly?

a.text: When business rules or workflow transitions require follow-up actions, such as missing information or insurance outreach.
a.explanation: Many tasks are rule-generated based on workflow checks.

b.explanation: Login events do not generate tasks.

c.explanation: Inventory alerts are not Scriptly tasks.

d.text: When a drug is discontinued nationally.
d.explanation: Drug status changes do not auto-create Scriptly tasks.

cards.explanation: Tasks are often auto-created based on workflow rules and triggers.

cards.question: Which object assigns Tasks to specific work queues within Scriptly?

a.explanation: Each task routes into an appropriate queue.

b.explanation: Drug config does not assign tasks.

d.explanation: Label templates format printed output.

cards.explanation: Tasks route into categorized queues according to business logic and templates.

cards.question: What happens when a Task is marked as completed?

a.text: The workflow may advance to the next stage if the task was a gating requirement.
a.explanation: Many statuses depend on task completion.

c.text: The patient is automatically discharged from the program.
c.explanation: Programs are not controlled via Tasks.

d.text: All related tasks are closed regardless of status.
d.explanation: Tasks do not auto-close siblings.

cards.explanation: Tasks may be workflow prerequisites, enabling stage advancement upon completion.

cards.question: Which type of Task is typically created when clinical documentation is needed from a prescriber?

a.explanation: Used for requesting missing clinical info.

b.explanation: Dispensing is separate from clinical outreach.

d.explanation: Unrelated to prescriber documentation.

cards.explanation: HCP outreach tasks request clinical data needed for PA, appeals, or clarification.

cards.question: Which Task category most often triggers PA-related follow-ups?

a.explanation: These support PA submission requirements.

d.explanation: Routing tasks occur downstream.

cards.explanation: PA workflows rely on documentation tasks to complete submission packets.

cards.question: Why do Task Templates often include default assignment rules?

a.text: To ensure tasks are routed to the correct operational team automatically.
a.explanation: Templates route tasks to appropriate queues.

b.text: To require all tasks be completed by pharmacists.
b.explanation: Tasks may be fulfilled by various roles.

c.text: To assign tasks randomly to distribute workload.

d.explanation: Templates rely on queue logic—they do not bypass it.

cards.explanation: Templates embed routing logic to support workflow consistency.

cards.question: What is the purpose of SLA priorities in Tasks?

a.text: To indicate urgency and required completion time for operational compliance.
a.explanation: SLA priority guides workload sequencing.

b.explanation: Eligibility is unrelated to task priority.

d.text: To specify the drug’s therapeutic class.
d.explanation: Therapeutic class is not tied to tasks.

cards.explanation: SLA priority dictates how quickly tasks must be completed.

cards.question: Which Task type is typically associated with obtaining missing insurance information?

a.explanation: These tasks are used to contact insurers or obtain missing plan details.

d.explanation: Unrelated to insurance outreach.

cards.explanation: Insurance outreach tasks support BI and IV processes requiring insurer contact.

cards.question: Which Task type is most commonly created when patient demographic information is missing?

a.explanation: Created to collect missing patient information.

b.explanation: Billing does not resolve demographic issues.

d.explanation: Shipping is downstream.

cards.explanation: Patient outreach tasks gather missing information needed for workflow progression.

cards.question: Which statement best describes a multi-step Task?

a.text: A task with embedded subtasks or checklists that must be completed before marking the task complete.
a.explanation: Some tasks represent multi-action workflows.

b.text: A task that automatically recreates itself upon completion.

c.text: A task that requires pharmacist approval only.

d.explanation: All tasks route through queues.

cards.explanation: Multi-step tasks often use checklists or sequential subtasks.

cards.question: Which operation most commonly generates Follow-Up Tasks?

a.explanation: Follow-up tasks track retries and escalation.

d.explanation: Fax routing is not a task trigger.

cards.explanation: Failed outreach often requires structured follow-up tasks.

cards.question: Why might Task dependencies be configured?

a.text: To ensure prerequisite steps are completed before another task can begin.
a.explanation: Dependencies ensure correct workflow sequencing.

b.text: To allow tasks to be completed in any order.

c.explanation: Permissions are not managed via tasks.

d.explanation: Queues remain essential.

cards.explanation: Some tasks cannot start until upstream actions are complete.

cards.question: Which team is most likely to receive a PA Documentation Task by default?

a.explanation: They manage PA documentation and submission.

d.explanation: Management may oversee but not perform PA tasks.

cards.explanation: PA tasks route to PA specialists by template logic.

cards.question: Which Task type is commonly created when a PA denial occurs?

a.explanation: Denials often require appeals.

b.explanation: Shipping does not occur during denials.

d.explanation: Financial programs are unrelated to denial itself.

cards.explanation: Appeal preparation tasks gather necessary documentation for resubmission.

cards.question: Which Task is most frequently used when insurance requests additional justification?

a.explanation: This provides supplemental clinical data.

d.explanation: Billing reconciliation is financial, not clinical.

cards.explanation: Insurers often require additional clinical detail for PA or Appeal decisions.

cards.question: What kind of Task is created when a prior authorization form must be faxed to a prescriber?

a.explanation: Faxing PA forms is a common task.

d.explanation: Shipping is unrelated.

cards.explanation: Fax tasks are generated when prescriber action is needed via fax.

cards.question: Which Task is typically created when patient affordability documentation is incomplete?

a.explanation: FA requires collecting income/insurance details.

d.explanation: Appeals occur for PA denials, not FA.

cards.explanation: FA outreach tasks gather missing affordability data.

cards.question: Which statement best describes a Task Queue?

a.text: A grouping of tasks assigned to a specific team or workflow category.
a.explanation: Queues organize operational workload.

b.text: A set of PA denial reasons.

c.text: A list of drugs associated with the Hub.

d.text: A record of patient payment history.
d.explanation: Payment history does not use queues.

cards.explanation: Queue placement determines which team works the task.

cards.question: Which Task type would be triggered by missing chart notes?

a.explanation: Chart notes are requested via clinical documentation tasks.

c.explanation: Chart notes have nothing to do with inventory.

d.explanation: Shipping occurs after approvals.

cards.explanation: Chart notes are clinical documentation requirements.

cards.question: Which Task type typically appears in compliance workflows?

a.explanation: Compliance uses audit tracking.

d.explanation: Physical workflows differ.

cards.explanation: Compliance often generates audit tasks for review and documentation.

cards.question: Which Task is created when a prescriber clarifies or corrects an ePrescribing error?

a.explanation: Clarification tasks resolve discrepancies.

d.explanation: Call QA is unrelated.

cards.explanation: Clarification tasks address missing or contradictory prescription details.

cards.question: Which action most commonly causes Tasks to be requeued?

a.text: A change in workflow status requiring a different operational team to handle the task.
a.explanation: Routing changes often requeue tasks.

c.explanation: Inventory processes do not affect task queues.

d.explanation: Reprints do not cause requeues.

cards.explanation: Task routing responds to workflow transitions and team assignments.

cards.question: Which Task type is most common at the start of the Benefits Investigation workflow?

a.explanation: BI often begins with verification tasks.

d.explanation: Intervention tasks occur later if needed.

cards.explanation: Insurance verification tasks confirm benefits and restrictions.

cards.question: What distinguishes a Task from a Status/Substatus in Scriptly?

a.text: Tasks represent actions to complete; Status/Substatus represents the workflow stage.
a.explanation: Statuses describe where the case is; tasks describe what needs to be done.

b.text: Tasks store clinical data; Status stores financial data.

c.text: Tasks are only visible to managers; statuses are hidden.

d.explanation: Status changes do not auto-complete tasks.

cards.explanation: Statuses reflect workflow state; tasks reflect actionable items within that state.

cards.question: Why do some Tasks require mandatory notes?

a.text: To document actions for compliance, auditing, or payer justification.
a.explanation: Documentation is required in regulated workflows.

d.explanation: Notes serve operational needs, not quotas.

cards.explanation: Mandatory notes ensure traceability and compliance.

cards.question: Which statement best describes an escalated Task?

a.text: A task reassigned to a higher-tier queue due to urgency, SLA violation, or repeated failed attempts.
a.explanation: Escalation is based on urgency or workflow blockage.

b.text: A task sent to the pharmacist automatically.

c.text: A task that cannot be completed.

d.text: A task that cancels all related tasks.
d.explanation: Escalated tasks do not cancel siblings.

cards.explanation: Escalation ensures time-sensitive or stalled tasks receive prompt attention.
